q: In a circular motion, the object just moves in a circle.
In rotational motion, the object rotates about an axis.
A rigid body is a body that deforms or changes shape during motion.
For a rigid body, the distance between any two particles within the body remains constant regardless of how the body moves.
In rotational motion, all particles constituting the rigid body undergo circular motion about a common axis.
If the angular velocity of an object is changing, it has an angular acceleration.
Angular acceleration is considered constant if the angular velocity changes at a constant rate.
The kinematic equations for rotational motion can be derived in terms of angular displacement, angular velocity, and angular acceleration.
The equations of motion for constant angular acceleration are entirely different from those for linear motion.
In rotational motion, angular displacement is represented by the symbol $\theta$.
The initial angular speed of an object at $t = 0$ is denoted by $\omega$.
The average angular speed $\bar{\omega}$ is calculated as $\frac{\omega + \omega_0}{2}$.
All particles of an object rotating around an axis of rotation move in a circular path.
The centre of rotation for particles in a rigid body lies on the axis of rotation.
The instantaneous angular velocity $\omega$ is different for different particles comprising a rotating rigid body.
The direction of linear velocity for a particle in rotational motion is tangent to the path.
Linear velocity is also referred to as tangential velocity in the context of rotational motion.
If the magnitude of angular velocity is constant, the object still has a tangential acceleration.
The tangential acceleration $a_T$ is always tangent to the circular path.
Tangential acceleration is a measure of how fast the tangential velocity changes.
The direction of the angular velocity vector is parallel to the plane of rotation.
The direction of angular velocity is usually specified by the right-hand rule.
When the angular velocity is increasing, the angular acceleration vector points in the opposite direction to the angular velocity.
If the angular velocity is decreasing, the angular acceleration vector points in the opposite direction to the angular velocity.
An object in uniform circular motion moves at a constant speed $v$.
In uniform circular motion, the magnitude of the velocity is constantly changing.
The change in direction of velocity in circular motion constitutes an acceleration.
An object revolving in a circle is continuously accelerating even if its speed remains constant.
The centripetal acceleration vector points away from the centre of the circular path.
Centripetal acceleration is also known as radial acceleration.
The linear velocity vector in uniform circular motion is tangential to the path.
Centripetal acceleration and linear velocity are parallel to each other at every point in the path.
In nonuniform circular motion, an object moves in a circular path with a varying speed.
In nonuniform circular motion, the object experiences angular acceleration.
Tangential acceleration is present in uniform circular motion.
Centripetal acceleration is present in both uniform and nonuniform circular motion.
In nonuniform circular motion, the resultant acceleration is the vector sum of centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The centripetal acceleration vector and tangential acceleration vector are perpendicular to each other.
The magnitude of resultant acceleration $a$ is given by $a = a_c + a_T$.
